segments.s342-g33: 'ز لشکر' is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from the army' (ز = from). 'هر آنکس که' is an indefinite relative clause 'whoever/whomsoever', with که introducing the clause. 'بد پیشرو' is an adjectival phrase (بد before the noun in poetic word order) meaning 'ill-/poorly-leading' or 'a poor vanguard' that modifies آنکس.
segments.s342-g34: ورا خواندی is a past narrative clause: ورا ('to him/unto him') + خواندی (an older poetic past of 'call/summon') meaning 'they summoned him/they called him there'. The following phrase 'شاه گشتاسپ گو' is an address: شاه گشتاسپ is the name/title 'King Gushtasp' and گو functions as a marker of speech/utterance (here equivalent to addressing him 'O King Gushtasp').
segments.s342-g35: بخواندند is past plural 'they summoned' (b- prefixed narrative form); و is the coordinating 'and'; نزدیک بنشاندند is a coordinated clause 'they seated (him) near', where بنشاندند is past plural 'they caused to sit / they seated'. The subject 'they' is the same across the coordinated verbs.
segments.s342-g36: ز هر جایگاهی is an ablative/adverbial phrase 'from every place / from every station'; سخن راندند is a poetic collocation 'they drove forth speech' meaning 'they delivered/exchanged words' or 'they spoke', with راندند as past plural used transitively with سخن as object.
segments.s342-g37: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'thus said/so spoke'. زیشان یکی نامور is inverted word order: زیشان ('of them/among them') + یکی ('a certain one / someone') + نامور (adjective 'renowned'), i.e. 'one renowned among them'. The whole segment means 'thus spoke one celebrated among them.'
segments.s342-g38: به گشتاسپ kای is an address to the king: به ('to/for') + گشتاسپ (Gushtasp). کای is an archaic vocative/title (equivalent to 'O' or the Kayanian title 'Kay') placed before the name. 'گرد زرین کمر' is an epithet attached to the name: گرد ('around/round') + زرین ('golden') + کمر ('girdle/waist'), i.e. 'golden-girdled', describing Gushtasp.
segments.s342-g39: ستاره‌شناسان ایران گروه is a nominal phrase 'the stargazers/astrologers of Iran, a group/company' with گروه appositive to ستاره‌شناسان ایران. The word order is noun + geographic/genitive qualifier + apposition.
segments.s342-g40: هرانکس که دانیم is 'whichever/whomever that we deem/know' — هرانکس که is the indefinite relative 'whoever', دانیم is present-subjunctive/indicative first-person-plural 'we know/consider'. دانش‌پژوه is an appositive noun 'scholar / student' describing such persons. The clause sets up identification of learned men among the group.
segments.s342-g97: چو here is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. تنها شوم = 'I become alone' (شوم = 1st‑person present/subjunctive 'I become' used narratively). ننگ دارم is a predicate 'I have shame / I am ashamed' (دارم = have). همی is the archaic/poetic habitual/emphatic particle ('always/still'), attached to the clause to show continuing feeling.
segments.s342-g98: ز = 'because of / from' giving the reason. لهراسپ names Lohrasp (proper noun). دل‌تنگ دارم is a compound predicate meaning 'I am heart‑sore / I pine' (دل‌تنگ + داشتن = to feel longing/sorrow). همی again marks the continued/habitual nature of the feeling.
segments.s342-g99: دل او = 'his heart' (subject). به کاوسیانست is a poetic contraction (کاوسیان است) meaning 'is toward / belongs to the Kausians' (به = toward/for). شاد is the predicate adjective 'happy/glad'. Word order is compressed/poetic: subject — indirect‑object/relationship phrase — predicate.
segments.s342-g100: نیاید is a negative present/optative 'does not come / does not arise'. It negates the following noun phrase: 'گذر مهر او بر نژاد' = 'the passage/transfer of his affection onto the lineage' (گذر = passing, مهر = love/affection, بر = onto/toward, نژاد = race/lineage). The construction says this transfer/extension of love does not occur.
segments.s342-g101: چو یک تن بود = 'when there was one person' (چو = when; یک تن = one person; بود = was, past). کم کند خواستار is a compact clause: کم = few/scant, کند = 'becomes/does' (3rd person), خواستار = 'suitors/desirers'; together 'suitors become few' — a poetically compressed subjectless or impersonal statement.
segments.s342-g102: چه داند = rhetorical interrogative 'what/ how does he know' (here 'who would know' / 'how could he know'). که introduces the subordinate clause. من چون شدم شهریار = 'that I, how, became king' (چون = 'how', شدم = became, شهریار = king). The clause asks rhetorically who can know the manner/reason of the speaker's rise to kingship.
segments.s342-g1: همی is a progressive/continuous marker here ('kept on/was'); رفت is the past stem meaning 'went/rode'; the subject گشتاسپ follows the verb as is common in older Persian poetry. پرتاب و خشم is a nominal phrase coordinated by و meaning 'impetuosity and anger' (پرتاب used as a noun denoting violent thrust/impetus).
segments.s342-g2: This is a pair of parallel noun-phrases joined by و: دلی پر ز کین = 'a heart full of hatred' and (چشم) پر از آب = '(his) eyes full of water' (i.e. tears). Note the poetic use of ز for از and the adjective-like construction 'پر ز/پر از' meaning 'full of'. چشم is conceptually supplied in the second half, so the second 'پر از آب' modifies 'چشم'.
segments.s342-g3: همی تاخت = 'he kept galloping' (همی marks continued action; تاخت is past 'galloped/rushed'). تا introduces the limit 'until' and the clause completes with رسید placed at the end: 'until (he) reached before Kabul' (پیش کابل is the locative phrase 'in front of Kabul').
segments.s342-g4: A simple enumerative nominal phrase (درخت و گل و سبزه و آب) listing the things he beheld, followed by the past verb دید at the end, as typical in Persian: 'he saw trees, flowers, grass/greenery, and water.' Each noun is coordinated by و.
segments.s342-g5: بدان is the archaic fused form of به + آن meaning 'to/at that'; جای خرم = 'that joyful/pleasant place' (خرم adjectival after جای); فرود آمدند is the compound verb 'they descended/alighted' with آمدند as the past plural verb placed at the end.
segments.s342-g6: ببودند is an older past-form meaning 'they were/they remained' followed by the duration یک روز 'one day'. دم بر زدند is idiomatic: دم here = 'moment/brief time' and زدند is a past verb 'they struck/sounded/made (a movement)'; together it conveys 'and after a (short) moment they acted/moved on' (context determines exact nuance).
segments.s342-g7: همه کوهسارانش = 'all its mountains/mountainous regions' (کوهسارانش with possessive -ش); نخچیر بود is a predicate meaning 'were game/filled with prey' or 'were hunting grounds', with بود as the past copula placed at the end.
segments.s342-g8: به جوی آبها is a locative phrase 'by/at the streams of water'; چون introduces a simile 'like' and the following terms (می‌و شیر) are compared to the waters — i.e. 'the waters were like wine and milk.' The clause ends with بود as the copula. (می‌و here functions as a poetic liquid—'wine/ferment'—paired with شیر 'milk'.)
segments.s342-g25: زریر سپهبد is an apposition: proper name + title = “Zarir, the spahbed.” به پیش سپاه is a directional phrase where به + پیش + سپاه means “toward the front of the army” (به here marks motion toward; پیش functions as “in front of/before” and takes سپاه as its object). Word order (name + title + direction) is typical of epic Persian.
segments.s342-g26: This is a simile clause: چو + noun + verb. چو باد دمان اندر آمد ز راه literally “as/like the wind the hem came in from the road,” i.e. “he arrived like a gust of wind.” اندر آمد is past tense ‘came in’; ز راه is a postverbal prepositional phrase “from the road.” The poetic image uses دمان (hem/edge) metaphorically and the word order is inverted for effect.
segments.s342-g27: چو + noun + دید introduces a temporal clause: “when he saw Gushtasp.” را marks گشتاسپ as the definite direct object. گریان is an adverbial participle “weeping/with tears,” modifying the verb برفت. برفت is the past tense ‘went/advanced’ (the archaic/epic form with the prefix بر‑ instead of simple رفت).
segments.s342-g28: پیاده is an adverb/adjectival phrase “on foot.” بدو = به + او “to him/unto him.” روی بنهاد is an idiom: روی (face) + بنهاد (placed) = “he turned/laid his face (toward him),” i.e. respectfully faced or bowed to him. The final word تفت is problematic here (rare/uncertain reading) and does not fit usual syntax; it may be an interjection or corrupt transmission.
segments.s342-g29: جهان‌آفرین را ستایش گرفت uses را to mark جهان‌آفرین as the object of praise. ستایش گرفت is a light-verb idiom meaning “took up praise” = “offered/poured out praise” (گرفت here functions as ‘began/took up’). Word order is object (Creator) + nominal phrase (praise) + verb.
segments.s342-g30: به پیش برادر is a locative/presence phrase “in the presence of / before his brother” (به + پیش = “at/in front of”); نیایش گرفت again uses گرفت as a light verb meaning “began/undertook supplication” = “prayed.” The structure parallels the previous clause (object/setting + action).
segments.s342-g31: گرفتند is the 3pl past “they took/ seized.” یکدگر را is the direct object “one another” (را marks it). کنار here means “beside/next to” and indicates how/where they held or placed themselves. The short item مر is unclear in this context (probably a textual/enclitic element) and its function is uncertain in the manuscript reading.
segments.s342-g32: نشستند is the 3pl past ‘they sat down’ (here the lemma supplied in the wordlist is not the verbal sense; interpret as ‘sat’). شادان is a manner/adjectival phrase “joyful/gleeful,” and در آن مرغزار is the locative prepositional phrase “in that meadow.” Together: “they sat down, joyful, in that meadow.”
segments.s342-g9: شب تیره (literally ‘dark night’) is a personified subject: شب (night) modified by the adjective تیره (dark). می‌خواست is the past imperfect of خواستن (‘to want’) expressing a desire; it introduces a wished-for action (a subordinate clause). از + میگسار marks the source/agent involved (’from/at the cupbearer’), with از indicating 'from' or 'of' in a syntactic complement to می‌خواست.
segments.s342-g10: ببردند is a past-form verb used here as the desired action after می‌خواست—grammatically it has an optative/subjunctive flavor (‘they would take’ / ‘should take’). The verb precedes its object (a common classical Persian order). شمع is the direct object (‘candle’). از بر جویبار is a locative/source phrase: از (from) + بر (here 'by/at/upon') + جویبار (stream), meaning 'from/by the stream.'
segments.s342-g11: چو is a temporal subordinating particle here meaning 'when'. بفروخت is a past-tense form from فروختن literally 'parted with' / 'left' (used here as 'departed'). از introduces the origin 'from' and کوه گیتی فروز is a noun phrase where گیتی (world) + فروز (bright, illuminating) postmodifies کوه — 'the world-bright (or world-illuminating) mountain.'
segments.s342-g12: برفتند (they departed) is the main verb for this clause; گردان ('troop') is the noun associated with that verb (verb–subject order). ازآن 'from that' refers back to the previously mentioned place. بیشه 'grove' is the location, and با باز و یوز 'with hawk and cheetah' lists the hunting companions: با 'with' plus باز (here meaning 'hawk/falcon') and یوز 'cheetah' joined by و.
segments.s342-g13: همی is an adverb of continuing action ('continuously/kept on') modifying the verb تاخت (galloped). Classical Persian allows verb–subject order, so تاخت precedes its logical subject اسپ ('steed'). از پی او is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in pursuit of him' (از + پی = 'after/in the wake of' + او 'him'), and زریر is the proper name of the person being pursued.
segments.s342-g14: زمانی here indicates a span of time ('for a while'). به جایی is a locative phrase meaning 'to/at any place' (often rendered 'anywhere'). نیاسود is the negative past of آسودن ('to rest or be at ease'), so 'did not rest/was not at ease.' دیر modifies the verb phrase and means 'long' (of time) — together rendering 'did not rest long anywhere.'
segments.s342-g15: چو again means 'when' introducing a temporal clause. آواز اسپان is a genitive-like construction 'the sound/cry of the horses' (آواز + plural اسپان). برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose/was heard' (i.e. the sound rose up). ز راه is a simple prepositional phrase 'from the road.'
segments.s342-g16: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they departed' and agrees with گردان ('troop') which follows it in verb–subject order. ز introduces the origin 'from' and نخچیرگاه names the place 'the hunting-ground' they left.
segments.s342-g17: چو is the conjunction 'when'. بنهاد is the past of نهاد (here 'placed/laid') used in the fixed poetic phrase بنهاد گوش meaning 'turned/laid his ear' = 'pricked up his ear' — note the classical verb-before-subject order: بنهاد (verb) + گشتاسپ (subject) + گوش (object). اندر آن literally 'in/at that' points back to the preceding event.
segments.s342-g18: چنین گفت = 'thus said' (چنین adverbially 'thus'). گفت is the past verb 'said' (here the senses list given is nominal, so it does not match the verbal use). با + NP marks the addressee/recipient: با نامور مهتران = 'to/with the renowned chiefs'. مهتران is a poetic plural 'great ones/chiefs'.
segments.s342-g19: که introduces the content clause 'that...'. این جز = 'this except' with جز meaning 'except/only'. به آواز اسپ زریر is a circumstantial/genitive phrase 'by/to the cry/voice of Zarir's horse' (classical phrasing often omits the ezāfe marker).
segments.s342-g20: نماند here means 'there remained / was left' used negatively in the sense 'nothing remained except...'; the following که introduces an explanatory/appositive clause: 'that he (او) is truly (راست) the voice of a lion (آواز شیر)'. راست functions adverbially as 'true/truly', and the clause explains or emphasizes the nature of that cry.
segments.s342-g21: نه‌تنها is the correlative 'not only'. بیامدگر (a compound agentive) means 'one who has come' / 'newcomer/visitor' — here implying 'not merely a casual newcomer has arrived'. او آمدست is the perfective/past construction 'he has come' (classical آمدست rather than modern آمده است).
segments.s342-g22: که continues the correlative ('but/that') and introduces the stronger clause. با + لشکری جنگجو = 'with an army of warriors' (لشکری 'troops/army', modified by جنگجو 'warlike/warrior'); آمدست again is the past/perfect 'has come'. Together: 'but he has come with a warlike army.'
segments.s342-g23: هنوز اندرین بد که is a poetic formula meaning 'still/yet it was bad in this (sense) that...' — i.e. 'worse yet, that...'. گردی بنفش is a noun phrase 'a purple dust/cloud' (گردی 'dust' with the color adjective بنفش following in classical order).
segments.s342-g24: پدید آمد is a two-word verbal phrase 'appeared/rose up' (پدید 'appear' + آمد 'came'). و links the next noun phrase. پیل پیکر درفش is a compact descriptive group: 'پیل پیکر' = 'elephant-bodied' (an attributive compound) qualifying درفش 'banner/standard' — hence 'an elephant-sized/elephant-bodied banner' appeared.
segments.s342-g49: ترا is the old/poetic object/topic form 'you' (ت + را) with را serving as a topical/emphatic marker rather than a simple accusative; از پدر literally 'from your father' marks lineage or source; سربسر is an adverb meaning 'completely/throughout'; نیکویست is adjective نیکوی 'good/excellent' plus the copula -ست ('is') fused (poetic orthography keeps them together). Together: 'You are entirely good by/through your father.'
segments.s342-g50: ندانم is first-person present negative 'I do not know'; که introduces the content/interrogative clause ('what/that') here functioning as 'what reason' or 'why'; آزردن is the infinitive/masdar 'to offend/to vex' used nominally; از بهر is a prepositional idea 'for the sake of/for' (از here 'of/concerning' combined with بهر 'for'); چی 'what'; ست is the copula 'is'. The clause amounts to 'I do not know what is the point/reason of offending.'
segments.s342-g51: بدو 'to him' (dative clitic phrase) precedes the verb as is common in Persian; گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (poetic listing places verb before subject); گشتاسپ is the subject 'Gushtasp'; کای is vocative 'O Kay' (an address); نامجوی is an epithet 'name-seeking' or 'seeker of renown' applied in direct address. Word order is Verb - Subject - Vocative in this line.
segments.s342-g52: ندارم is first-person present negative 'I do not have' (ندارَم = I lack/have not); به پیش 'in the presence of / before' (prepositional phrase indicating facing someone); پدر 'father'; آبروی 'reputation/honor' (direct object). Together: 'I have no reputation before my father.' Note Persian places the negated possessive verb (ندارم) before the prepositional phrase that indicates whose presence.
segments.s342-g53: به کاوسیان is a dative/benefactive phrase 'for Kay Kavus' (کاوسیان = Kay Kavus, here in the dative sense); خواهد is third-person present 'he wishes/wants'; او 'he' is the intended beneficiary/subject of that wishing; نیکوی is adjective 'good/excellent' (adjectival predicate following the verb 'wants' to express the wished quality). The clause means roughly 'He desires for Kay Kavus that he be excellent.'
segments.s342-g54: بزرگی 'greatness' is a noun that continues the description of the wished excellence; و هم 'and also' links a second wished item; افسر خسروی is a compound 'royal helmet/crown' (افسر here 'helmet/crown', خسروی 'kingly/royal'), so the whole phrase lists 'greatness and also the royal crown' as things he wishes (for the addressee).
segments.s342-g55: اگر is the conditional 'if'; تاج ایران is a genitive noun phrase 'the crown of Iran' (تاج + ایران); سپارد is third-person singular present 'he entrusts / hands over' (سپردن); به من 'to me' marks the recipient. This subordinate conditional clause sets up the result in the next segment.
segments.s342-g56: پرستش 'worship' is a verbal noun; کنم is first-person subjunctive/optative 'I will do / I would do' used here as the result/future of the conditional; چون 'like/as' introduces a simile; بتان را 'the idols' marked by را as the object of پرستش; شمن 'the sorcerer' (agent of the simile). The clause means 'I will worship (it) like the sorcerer [worships] his idols'—the verb in the simile is omitted (elliptical) but understood from پرستش کنم.
segments.s342-g57: وگرنه introduces the conditional/contrast ("if not/otherwise"); نباشم is the negative present/subjunctive first‑person form of بودن (here meaning "I should not be / I will not be"). به درگاه اوی is a locative phrase: درگاه means the royal court/presence and اوی is an archaic enclitic third‑person possessive meaning "his". Word order places the negated verb before the locative phrase for a terse poetic cadence.
segments.s342-g58: ندارم is the first‑person present of داشتن with negation ("I do not have / I have no"). دل روشن is a noun + postpositive adjective construction meaning "a bright/enlightened heart." از marks source or cause ("from, because of") and ماه اوی — literally "his moon" — is a poetic epithet (the beloved's/sovereign's radiant face or moonlike beauty); اوی again is the archaic possessive "his."
segments.s342-g59: به جایی شوم is a conditional/optative clause: به + جایی ("to/some place") + شوم (first‑person subjunctive/optative of شدن, "may I become/go"). کم modifies the verb as an adverb meaning "scarcely/rarely," and نیابند is the third‑person plural negative/subjunctive form of یافتن (here "they do not find / may not find"). نیز adds the sense "also/too" or emphasis at the end; overall the clause means "if I go somewhere, they will hardly find me."
segments.s342-g60: به لهراسپ مانم: به + proper name (لهراسپ) plus مانم (first‑person present/subjunctive of ماندن) — "I remain/stay with/to Lohrasp." همه مرز و چیز is a concise listing of territory/possessions: همه = "all", مرز = "borders/frontiers", و چیز = "and things/possessions"; together, "I will remain with Lohrasp — all borders and possessions (are with him / I remain with him alongside all the realm)."
segments.s342-g61: بگفت is the simple past/reporting verb "he said." این refers to the just‑spoken remark. و is the coordinating conjunction "and/then." برگشت is the past verb "returned." زان is a contraction meaning "from that" (ز + آن) marking origin, and مرغزار is the place "meadow/pasture." The sequence narrates: "He said this and returned from that meadow."
segments.s342-g62: بیامد is the past tense "came." بر here functions as a directional preposition "to/toward." نامور شهریار is a rolled epithet: نامور (renowned) qualifying شهریار (king) — "to/unto the renowned king."
segments.s342-g63: چو introduces a temporal clause "when." بشنید is the past tense "he heard." لهراسپ is the subject (Lohrasp). با مهتران is an accompaniment phrase: با = "with" and مهتران is the plural of مهتر (chiefs/elders) — an archaic title; together "he, with the chiefs,..."
segments.s342-g64: پذیره شدش is a compact classical construction: پذیره (reception/acceptance) + شد + clitic ش (object pronoun) — here effectively "he received him / he was received him" in context meaning Lohrasp welcomed/received the visitor. با سپاهی گران: با = "with," سپاهی = "troops/soldiers," گران (literally "heavy/valuable") functions here as an adjective meaning "mighty/strong/ numerous," so the phrase means "with a mighty army."
segments.s342-g81: بیاراست is a past (perfective) verb meaning 'adorned/arranged' and here governs the following noun phrase as its object. The noun ایوان (iwan/porch/hall) comes next, followed by the adjective/epithet گوهرنگار ('jewel‑studded') in postpositive position — a common poetic order. This short clause is a complete predicate that links to the next clause by continuing the recounting of what they did.
segments.s342-g82: نهادند is a past plural verb 'they placed/put' (here: set out). خوان is the direct object 'banquet/table of food'. و simply coordinates 'and'. می‌خوشگوار is an attributive phrase describing the wine ('pleasant/delightful wine'); the mi‑ prefix is part of a traditional formation for wine phrases. Together this clause continues the series of actions begun in the previous clause.
segments.s342-g83: یکی جشن کردند: یکی ('one' / 'a') precedes جشن ('festival/celebration') and کردند is the past plural 'they did/held'. کز introduces a subordinate clause and here means 'from/at which' (introducing what followed upon that celebration). چرخ is the poetic word for 'sky/heaven' and ماه 'moon' stands in the subordinate clause as a conspicuous celestial actor — the clause continues into the next segment.
segments.s342-g84: ستاره is the (collective) subject 'star/stars' and ببارید is the past intransitive 'rained/fell' (poetic: 'showered down'). بر is the preposition 'upon' and جشنگاه is the object 'place/scene of celebration'. The verb phrase describes the stars (and implicitly the moon) seeming to rain down upon the festival.
segments.s342-g85: چنان بد ز مستی که is a result clause opener: چنان ('so') + بد (here adverbial 'badly/strongly') + ز مستی ('from drunkenness') + که ('that') introduces the consequence. هر مهتری = 'each/every chief' (هر modifies مهتری). The clause prepares a consequence describing what happened because of their intoxication.
segments.s342-g86: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they went/proceeded' used here for the resulting action; the sequence بر + سر marks location 'upon the head', while ز + زر + افسری describes the material/thing ('of gold a diadem'). Syntactically the clause says that (as a result) they put/placed a golden diadem on the head (i.e., crowned or set a helmet‑like ornament).
segments.s342-g87: به کاوسیان is a prepositional phrase indicating association or location ('among/for the Kausians'). بود is the past copula 'was'. لهراسپ (Lohrasp) follows the verb (inverted order for emphasis) and شاد is the predicate adjective 'glad/merry'. The clause therefore states Lohrasp's mood among the Kausians.
segments.s342-g88: همیشه is the adverb 'always/ever'. ز before a name here means 'of/about' or 'on account of' and introduces کیخسروش (Kay Khosrow) as the object of remembrance. بود is again the past copula 'was' and یاد is 'remembrance/memory'. Together the clause says that the remembrance of Kay Khosrow was always present.
segments.s342-g73: ز شاهی is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'of/from kingship'. مرا (here segmented as م + را) functions as a dative/emphatic element 'for me' (old/poetic order). نام تاجست is a nominal predicate where تاجست = تاج + است ('is crown'); the copula follows the predicate (poetic/normative word order). The final و تخت simply adds 'and throne' as a second predicate item.
segments.s342-g74: ترا is the dative/possessive 'to you' (here written as ت + را); it marks the person who possesses or receives the list that follows. مهر و فرمان و پیمان و بخت is a paratactic list (connected by repeated و) giving items belonging/pertaining to 'you' — 'love/affection, command, covenant, and fortune/destiny.' The verb 'is' is understood from the previous clause (ellipsis), so this is an asyndetic clause attached to the first.
segments.s342-g75: ورا is the indirect-object/dative 'to him' (poetic placement before the verb). گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here used with the speaker گشتاسپ as subject); the name گشتاسپ is the subject. کای شهریار is a vocative: کای = 'O' (address marker) + شهریار = 'sovereign/king.'
segments.s342-g76: منم is 'I am' (first-person copula/affirmation). بر درت uses the preposition بر ('on/at/toward') + در + ـت (your) to mean 'at your door/threshold' (possessive suffix -t). The second بر repeats the preposition and introduces یکی پیشکار = 'with/attended by one attendant' (یکی = one / a certain; پیشکار = attendant), so the whole phrase means 'I am at your door with one attendant.'
segments.s342-g77: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. کم کنی is present-subjunctive (second-person) of کردن used here in the sense 'you lessen/shorten' (i.e., reduce); its object is جاه 'rank/status' placed after the verb (poetic word order). فرمان کنم is the main clause: فرمان + کنم ('I will issue/order'); کنَم is 1st-person subjunctive/volitional often used for future or deliberate action.
segments.s342-g78: به پیمان is a prepositional phrase 'by/in accordance with the covenant' (به = by/with, پیمان = covenant/treaty). روان را is the direct object (روان plus object marker را) — here treated as the person/entity to be given; گروگان کنم is 'I will make/take (as) a hostage' (گروگان = hostage; کنم = 'I will do/make'), completing the threat promised in the previous clause.
segments.s342-g79: بزرگان is the plural subject 'the great ones / elders / nobles'. برفتند is the past-tense plural verb 'they went/departed'. با او is the prepositional complement 'with him', and به راه is the direction 'on/along the road'. Word order (subject — verb — adjuncts) is straightforward here.
segments.s342-g80: گرازان و پویان is a coordinated nominal phrase — either literal animals ('boars') paired with 'the active/moving ones' or (more likely in epic diction) the names/titles of groups or persons (both readings are possible). به ایوان شاه is a locative/directional phrase 'to the king's iwan/portico/hall' (ایوان شاه = the king's porch/hall).
segments.s342-g65: Subject (جهانجوی, 'the world-seeker') comes first, followed by the noun phrase روی پدر ('the face of the father') — in poetic Persian the possessive relation is written as noun + possessor without an ezafe. دید is the past tense verb 'saw/beheld' and باز (here postverbal) is the adverb 'again'.
segments.s342-g66: فرود آمد is a composite verbal idea 'descended / came down' (فرود + آمد). از باره is a small prepositional/temporal phrase 'on that occasion / from that matter' (here 'on that occasion'). بردش is an older past form of بردن with enclitic -ش ('him'); in this line it means 'took/led him' (the supplied senses did not include this verb sense). نماز is the object 'prayer' (he led him into prayer).
segments.s342-g67: ورا is an object/pronoun phrase 'to him' (or 'him'); تنگ here is adverbial 'close / tightly'; لهراسپ is the subject (the king Lohrasp); در برگرفت (برگرفت) is a past verb 'took up / seized / embraced' — together the phrase means 'Lohrasp embraced him tightly.' Word order is flexible and poetic: object pronoun + manner + subject + verb.
segments.s342-g68: بدان is a prepositional phrase 'to/with that' (here 'with that'); پوزش is 'apology/forgiveness' and آرایش is 'appearance/attitude' (sense of 'makeup/appearance' used here); اندر گرفت (گرفت with اندر) is the verbal idiom 'took on / assumed.' Altogether: 'he assumed an apologetic appearance with that apology.' Note: the input tokenization split بدان oddly (b + دان); the second token in the data does not match the sense used here, so that token is marked accordingly.
segments.s342-g69: که ... باد frames an optative/wish clause ('may ... be'). تاج تو is 'your crown' and is repeated appositively as تاج سر ماه ('the crown upon the moon's head/brow') for poetic emphasis. باد at the end is the optative auxiliary 'may.'
segments.s342-g70: ز تو 'from you / away from you' (ز is the poetic form of از). دیو را دست کوتاه باد is another optative clause: را marks دیو as the affected topic, دست کوتاه literally 'a short hand' = 'restricted power/reach'; باد again is 'may' — 'may the demon's hand be short toward you' i.e. 'may demons have no power over you.'
segments.s342-g71: که introduces a wish/hope clause again. هرگز 'never' negates the verb. نیاموزدت is an older/perfect past form with a second-person enclitic (-ت) meaning 'he did not teach you' but in this poetic context functions as a jussive/optative ('may he never teach you'). راه بد is the object 'the bad/evil way.'
segments.s342-g72: چو is a temporal/conditional 'when/as/if'. دستور بد is 'evil counsel/command.' بر در شاه is a locative phrase 'upon/at the king's door/gate' (بر + در 'door' used idiomatically). The final بد reiterates the negative/evil quality — the whole clause gives the circumstance ('as when evil counsel comes before the king').
segments.s342-g41: به اخترت is a dative phrase ('to/for your star'); اخترت is the classical possessive form (اختر + enclitic -ت = 'your star'). گویند is an older third‑person plural present 'they say' introducing reported speech. کیخسروی here is an epithet/appositive ('like Keykhosrow' or 'of Keykhosrow') that begins the reported prediction.
segments.s342-g42: به شاهی uses به + noun to indicate entering a state ('into kingship' = 'become king'). به تخت مهی is another to‑phrase 'onto the moon‑like (مهی) throne (تخت)'; word order is classical (state/place after the 'به'). بر شوی is an older form of شدن: شوی here functions as the third‑person (classical) verb 'become/ascend' with the preposition بر ('upon'), so the clause says 'you will mount/become upon' the throne.
segments.s342-g43: کنون is an adverb 'now/presently'. افسر شاه هندوستان is a genitive construction 'the helmet/crown of the king of Hindustan' (افسر here poetic for crown/helmet, modified by شاه هندوستان). Together they form the temporal/topic phrase 'now (the) helmet of the Indian king'.
segments.s342-g44: بپوشی is the classical/preverbal ب‑ + پوشی form of پوشیدن; in context it works as an imperative/subjunctive 'put on / you wear'. نباشیم is the first‑person plural negative form 'we are not' (classical usage), and همداستان is an adjective 'of one mind / like‑minded'. The clause states that the speaker(s) are not of the same opinion/agreeing.
segments.s342-g45: ازیشان = از + ایشان 'of/from them' marking the group; کسی نیست is an existential negative 'there is no one'; یزدان‌پرست is the predicate noun 'God‑worshipper' (pious person). The phrase says that among them there exists no pious person.
segments.s342-g46: یکی هم is an emphatic 'not even one' (یکی = one/somebody, هم = even/also), ندارند is third‑person plural 'they do not have', and با شاه دست is a prepositional phrase literally 'with the king hand' — idiomatically 'an ally at the king's side' (دست = power/influence). The whole clause states they have not a single person in the king's service/ally.
segments.s342-g47: نگر is an imperative 'look/consider.' تا here is temporal 'until' introducing the clause that follows: پسند آید 'approval/choice comes' (آید = 'comes', پسند = 'what is pleasing/approved'). اندر خرد is a locative/agent phrase 'in (one's) wisdom/reason' — classical word order puts the locative after the verb.
segments.s342-g48: کجا is 'where' (here functioning as a relative/interrogative connective: 'in what case/where'); رای را has the object marker را after رای ('the counsel/opinion' as a definite object). شاه is the subject 'the king' and فرمان برد is a verb phrase where برد is classical past/presence of بردن 'to take/carry' — overall asking/remarking about where the king will act on/take counsel. (Note: the provided sense list for برد does not include the verbal sense 'take/bring', so that sense is indicated below as unknown.)
segments.s342-g89: همی before a past verb marks iterative or continuous action in classical/epic Persian ('kept ...ing'). ریخت is the past of ریختن used here intransitively meaning 'poured/flowed'; زان ('from that') gives the source/cause, and درد گشتاسپ is a genitive construction 'the pain of Gushtasp' that supplies the cause of the bleeding. خون is the thing that flowed and sits after the cause (poetic word order).
segments.s342-g90: همی + گفت again frames repeated speech ('he kept saying'). گفت here functions as the reporting verb 'said/kept saying' (the provided noun-like sense list is mismatched with the verb use). هرگونه is an adverbial phrase 'in any way/every kind' modifying گفت, and با رهنمون is a prepositional phrase ('with/to the guide') indicating the addressee or the person he addresses.
segments.s342-g91: همی گفت introduces reported speech repeatedly. هرچند is a concessive conjunction 'although' that begins the subordinate clause. کوشم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive of کوشیدن ('I try' / 'I strive'), and به رای is a prepositional phrase 'by/with counsel' (به + رای) specifying the means or manner of that effort.
segments.s342-g92: نیارم here does not match the supplied sense list (the surface word functions as an archaic/poetic negative or inability—'I cannot/find no means') so its sense is flagged as unknown. همی again emphasizes continuing speech; چاره این به جای is a verbless clause where چاره ('remedy') is the predicate and این به جای ('this for this place') gives its applicability—the copula 'is' is omitted, as is common in Persian.
segments.s342-g93: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). با سواران is an adverbial phrase 'with the horsemen.' شوم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive form of شدن ('I may become / I become'), and مهتری follows as a predicate nominative 'a chief/leader' (poetic inversion places the predicate after the verb).
segments.s342-g94: فرستد is a 3rd‑person verb form ('he will/send[s]') used with future-like force in narrative; its subject is supplied from the context. پسم ('پس' with enclitic) means 'after/behind me' and is the target direction of the sending; نیز 'also' adds emphasis/also; با لشکری is a prepositional phrase 'with an army' indicating accompaniment or the force sent.
segments.s342-g95: به چاره is a prepositional phrase of purpose ('for/to a remedy'). ز ره (poetic order for از ره) means 'from the road/way' and indicates departure. بازگرداندم is 1st‑person past ('I turned back / I returned'); here it expresses that the narrator caused himself to return or simply 'I returned' in the narrative past.
segments.s342-g96: بسی is an intensifier 'much/many' modifying the following nouns. خواهش و پندها is a coordinated noun phrase 'entreaties (خواهش) and advices/admonitions (پندها)'; پندها is plural. راندم is 1st‑person past of راندن ('I drove/sent/uttered') used figuratively here to mean 'I sent forth / I uttered' those pleas and counsels.

sentences.s342-s17: From the army they summoned whoever had been a poor vanguard, and they called him there, addressing him “O King Gushtasp.”
sentences.s342-s18: They summoned him and seated him nearby; from every place they set forth speeches.
sentences.s342-s19: Thus spoke one renowned among them:
sentences.s342-s20: The Iran stargazers, a company — whomever among them we know as a scholar,
sentences.s342-s49: When I am alone I am ashamed; because of Lohrasp I am heart-sore.
sentences.s342-s50: His heart is glad for the Kausians; his affection does not pass on to the lineage.
sentences.s342-s51: When there was only one man, suitors grew few; who knows how I became king?
sentences.s342-s1: Gushtasp rode on, impetuous and angry; his heart full of hatred and his eyes full of tears.
sentences.s342-s2: He rode on until he came before Kabul, and he saw trees and flowers and greenery and water.
sentences.s342-s3: They alighted in that pleasant place; they stayed one day and then stirred/made a move.
sentences.s342-s4: All its mountain ranges were full of game; by the watercourses the waters were like wine and milk.
sentences.s342-s13: Zarir, the spahbed, came forward toward the army from the road like a gust of wind.
sentences.s342-s14: When he saw Gushtasp he went forward weeping; on foot he turned his face toward him.
sentences.s342-s15: He offered praise to the Creator, and before his brother he offered supplication.
sentences.s342-s16: They took one another, sat down beside one another, joyful in that meadow.
sentences.s342-s5: The dark night wished that the cupbearer should take the candle from beside the stream.
sentences.s342-s6: When they left the world-bright mountain, they set out from that grove with hawk and cheetah.
sentences.s342-s7: The steed kept galloping after him, Zarīr; for a while he did not rest long anywhere.
sentences.s342-s8: When the sound of horses came from the road, the troop set off from the hunting-ground.
sentences.s342-s9: When Gushtasp pricked up his ear at that, he said thus to the renowned chiefs.
sentences.s342-s10: that nothing remained here except the cry of Zarir’s horse; for he truly has the voice of a lion.
sentences.s342-s11: Not only has a mere newcomer come, but he has come with a warlike army.
sentences.s342-s12: Still worse in that moment was that a purple dust rose up, and an elephant-bodied banner appeared.
sentences.s342-s25: You are entirely noble from your father; I do not know what reason there is to offend (you).
sentences.s342-s26: Gushtasp said to him, 'O Kay, name-seeker,' and 'I have no reputation before my father.'
sentences.s342-s27: He wishes for Kay Kavus that he be excellent in greatness and also the royal crown (helmet).
sentences.s342-s28: If he entrusts the crown of Iran to me, I will worship (it) as a sorcerer does his idols.
sentences.s342-s29: If not, I will not be in his presence; my heart has no brightness from his moon.
sentences.s342-s30: Even if I go to some place, they will scarcely find me.
sentences.s342-s31: He said this and returned from that meadow.
sentences.s342-s32: When Lohrasp heard it, he, with the chiefs, received him with a mighty army.
sentences.s342-s41: They adorned the jewel‑studded iwan, and set out a banquet and delightful wine.
sentences.s342-s42: They held a festival, at which from the heavens the moon and stars seemed to fall upon the celebrating place.
sentences.s342-s43: They were so overcome with drunkenness that, for every chief, they placed a golden diadem upon the head.
sentences.s342-s44: Lohrasp was glad among the Kausians; the memory of Kay Khosrow was ever present.
sentences.s342-s37: As for kingship: for me its name is 'crown and throne'; for you it is 'affection, command, covenant, and fortune.'
sentences.s342-s38: Gushtasp said to him, 'O sovereign, I am at your door with one attendant.'
sentences.s342-s39: If you lessen his rank, I will give the order; by the covenant I will take Ravan as hostage.
sentences.s342-s40: The elders went with him on the road; Gorazan and Puyan (or the boar-troop and the Puyans) to the king's portico.
sentences.s342-s33: The world-seeker saw his father's face again; he came down and, on that occasion, led him in prayer.
sentences.s342-s34: Lohrasp clasped him close to himself, and with that apology assumed an apologetic bearing.
sentences.s342-s35: May your crown be the crown upon the moon's brow; may the demon's hand be kept short from you.
sentences.s342-s36: May he never teach you the evil path, as when evil counsel stands at the king's gate.
sentences.s342-s21: The astrologers say of your star that you will be a king like Keykhosrow — you will become king and mount the moon‑bright throne.
sentences.s342-s22: Now (you) wear the Indian king's helmet; we are not of one mind.
sentences.s342-s23: None of them is God‑worshipping; they do not even have a single person allied with the king.
sentences.s342-s24: Look (consider) until a choice pleasing to wisdom comes; where will the king take counsel (or follow the plan)?
sentences.s342-s45: From that pain Gushtasp's blood kept flowing; he kept saying all manner of things to the guide.
sentences.s342-s46: He kept saying, 'Although I try by counsel, I cannot; still, this remedy is fitting here.'
sentences.s342-s47: If I go with the horsemen and become a leader, he will send troops after me as well.
sentences.s342-s48: I turned back from the road to seek a remedy and uttered many entreaties and counsels.
